description: Docker explanation for a developer new to DevOps

turns.content: Can you explain Docker to me like I know programming but nothing about DevOps?

Docker solves the 'it works on my machine' problem. Instead of shipping code and hoping the recipient has the right Python version and libraries, you ship a container — a self-contained box that includes your code, its exact runtime, and all dependencies. That box runs identically on your laptop, your colleague's machine, and any cloud server.
How is that different from a virtual machine?

A virtual machine simulates an entire computer including a full operating system kernel. Docker containers share the host OS kernel and only package what is different — your app and its dependencies. Result: a Docker container starts in milliseconds and is typically 10-100MB. A VM takes minutes to boot and is typically gigabytes. You can run dozens of containers on a laptop; you can run maybe 2-3 VMs. VMs are still used for stronger security boundaries, but for most application deployment Docker is the practical choice.
What's a Dockerfile and how do I write a basic one for a Python FastAPI app?

A Dockerfile is the recipe for building your container image. For a FastAPI app:

FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

Build with: docker build -t my-app .
Run with: docker run -p 8000:8000 my-app

Two tips: use slim base images to keep size down, and copy requirements before your code so Docker caches the dependency layer — rebuilds are much faster.
